summary: My older brother was born in Tule Lake, and my parents were incarcerated there during World War II. My mother went into the camp pregnant, which makes me think about how difficult it must have been for her to receive medical care and raise a child in such harsh conditions. My parents were eventually able to leave Tule Lake by volunteering to work in the sugar beet fields. Visiting Tule Lake recently with my sisters helped me understand the reality of their experience in the camp.

I was born in Caldwell, Idaho, where my parents were assigned after leaving Tule Lake. Caldwell had a labor camp originally built for Dust Bowl migrant workers, and during the war, it became a place for Japanese Americans to work in sugar beet fields. My father became a supervisor there, and the camp had facilities like schools and playgrounds. The original camp buildings were torn down in the late '50s or '60s, but some structures like the post office and water tower still remain.

My parents got married on November 22, 1941, and just two weeks later, Pearl Harbor was attacked. This event changed everything for them, as they began their married life in a concentration camp. The attack marked the start of the internment and the hardships they faced as Japanese Americans during the war.

The Amalgamated Sugar Company played a key role in recruiting Japanese Americans from the camps to work in the sugar beet fields because there was a labor shortage due to the war. My parents volunteered for this work and were assigned to Caldwell, Idaho. The company lobbied for workers, which helped many families like mine get out of the camps and into seasonal labor.

Michael Dittenber is the director of the labor camp site in Caldwell, Idaho. When I visited, he gave me a detailed history and showed us around the remaining buildings like the post office and water tower. His knowledge helped me understand the living conditions and community life of Japanese Americans working there during the war.

Robert Matsui and his family were also in the labor camp where my parents were assigned. They were fortunate because once in that camp, they did not have to return to Tule Lake. My mother remembered the Matsui family being there, which connects our families' experiences during that difficult time.

Many of the young workers my parents supervised in the sugar beet fields came from places like San Francisco and Los Angeles. These young men often had no farming experience and were shocked by the hard labor. It was a big adjustment for them, and my parents would chuckle about their unfamiliarity with tools like hoes.

Similar to San Francisco, many young Japanese Americans from Los Angeles were recruited to work in the sugar beet fields during the war. They often had no prior experience with farm work, which surprised my parents who supervised them. This shows how the internment and labor programs affected people from different urban areas.

Grace Uyehara was a powerful lobbyist in Washington for redress and reparations for Japanese Americans. She was a mover and shaker who helped bring together coalitions to push for an official government apology. Her efforts were crucial in the success of the redress movement, especially from her base in Philadelphia.

entity: Presidential Commission on Wartime Relocation and Internment of Civilians
summary: The Presidential Commission on Wartime Relocation and Internment of Civilians was a turning point for our community. It brought attention to the injustices we faced and encouraged people to share their stories. Judge Marutani, the only Japanese American on the Commission and from Philadelphia, played an important role in this process, which eventually led to the government's apology.

Judge Marutani was the only Japanese American member of the Presidential Commission on Wartime Relocation and Internment of Civilians. He was from Philadelphia and was part of a strong group of people there who believed in seeking justice and redress. His presence on the Commission helped give our community a voice during that critical time.

Philadelphia was an important center for Japanese American redress efforts. People like Grace Uyehara and Judge Marutani were based there and helped push for the government apology. Many Japanese Americans settled in Philadelphia after the war, and the community there was active in supporting education and civil rights for Nikkei.

Jim Wright was a Senator from Texas who supported the redress movement. His backing was important because the 442nd Infantry Regiment, which rescued the Lost Battalion, was highly respected in Texas. The coalition working for redress strategically gained his support to help pass the legislation.

Texas played a role in the redress movement because of its respect for the 442nd Infantry Regiment, which rescued the Lost Battalion. This connection helped gain political support from figures like Senator Jim Wright. It was a key state in building the coalition that pushed for the government's apology to Japanese Americans.

The 442nd Infantry Regiment was a highly decorated Japanese American military unit during World War II. Their heroic rescue of the Lost Battalion earned them great respect, especially in places like Texas. This reputation helped the redress movement gain political allies who admired their service and sacrifice.

The Lost Battalion was a unit rescued by the 442nd Infantry Regiment during World War II. This event is well remembered and respected, particularly in Texas, and it helped Japanese Americans gain political support for redress. The bravery of the 442nd in this rescue became a symbol of loyalty and sacrifice.

After the war, my family returned to Auburn, Washington, where my father worked in his brother-in-law's pottery and became a supervisor. I grew up there, and it was a place where my parents could rebuild their lives. Auburn was home to many Japanese Americans who returned from the camps and tried to reintegrate into society.

Seabrook, New Jersey, was one of the places where Japanese Americans were recruited to work after the war. Some Nikkei who came through Seabrook later settled in Philadelphia and other East Coast areas. The Quakers and organizations like the American Friends Service Committee helped support these communities.

The American Friends Service Committee, a Quaker organization, was very helpful to Japanese Americans after the war. They helped arrange housing, education, and employment opportunities for Nikkei who resettled on the East Coast. A close friend of mine worked for them, and their support was crucial for many families.

The Nikkei community includes Japanese Americans who faced internment and discrimination during the war. Some Nikkei never went to camps, especially on the East Coast where they were fewer in number. My parents were Nisei, and their experiences show how the community was diverse and how assimilation varied by region.

My mother grew up in Burlington, Washington, a small town north of Seattle where only three Japanese families lived. Despite being a small minority, they assimilated well into the community and were middle class. My grandparents owned a dry cleaning business there, and the families were integrated into local social life.

Before the war, migrant workers from Arkansas and Oklahoma who fled the Dust Bowl lived in camps like the one in Caldwell, Idaho. These camps were low-income housing created by the federal government for displaced families during the Great Depression. Later, when the war caused labor shortages, Japanese Americans were brought in to work these fields.

The Great Depression forced many families from the Dust Bowl region to move west and live in government-created camps like Caldwell, Idaho. These camps provided low-income housing for displaced workers. During World War II, these camps became sites for Japanese American laborers after the original migrant workers moved on to war industries.

My mother graduated from the University of Washington with a BA in Home Economics. Despite her education, she was told by a college counselor that there were no jobs for women like her, especially women of color. Her experience reflects the limited opportunities available to Japanese American women before the war.

Pendleton, Oregon, was mentioned as part of the region where Japanese Americans lived and worked before the war. Although not much detail was given, it is part of the broader Pacific Northwest context of my family's history and the Japanese American experience in that area.
